title: Raising chicks to point of lay
subtitle: The full arc from hatch day to first egg.
excerpt: From hatch to first egg takes around 18 to 22 weeks depending on breed. The arc divides naturally into three phases: brooding heat (weeks 1 to 5), outdoor transition (weeks 5 to 10), and grow-on (weeks 10 to point of lay). Each phase has distinct temperature, feed, and housing requirements. Get the transitions right and losses are minimal.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018). BHWT chick-rearing guide, bhwt.org.uk. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The age at which a pullet begins laying eggs, typically 18 to 22 weeks for hybrids and 20 to 26 weeks for traditional breeds. A pullet at point of lay shows a red comb and wattles, squats for the keeper when stroked, and explores nest boxes.
A finely milled high-protein (20%) feed for chicks from hatch to around 8 weeks. The crumb size is small enough for a day-old chick to swallow and digest. Switch to growers' pellets at 8 weeks.

projectSchedule.title: Hatch day
projectSchedule.body: Transfer chicks from hatcher to brooder. Heat plate set at 37°C; floor temperature under plate should be 35°C.

projectSchedule.title: Week 1 check
projectSchedule.body: Check all chicks are feeding, drinking, and spreading away from the heat source during the day. Reduce heat plate height slightly.

projectSchedule.title: Two-week check
projectSchedule.body: Reduce brooder temperature by 3°C. Check for pasty vent. Clean gently with warm water if found.

projectSchedule.title: Off heat
projectSchedule.body: Switch to chick crumb twice daily with free water access in a run. Most hybrids are fully feathered and need no supplemental heat above 16°C by week 5.

projectSchedule.title: Switch to growers
projectSchedule.body: Move from chick crumb to growers pellets at 8 weeks. Chick crumb has too much calcium for older birds.

projectSchedule.title: Pre-lay transition
projectSchedule.body: Introduce layer pellets over one week mixed with growers. Red combs, squatting, and nest box exploration signal first egg is close.

projectSchedule.title: First egg expected (hybrid)
projectSchedule.body: Most laying hybrids produce their first egg between weeks 18 and 20. Traditional breeds typically 20 to 26 weeks.

content.text: Hatching your own chicks and raising them to

is the most involved route to a productive flock and the one that gives you the most control over the birds' early development. The critical inputs are heat in the first five weeks, the right feed at each stage, and protection from disease pressure at the outdoor transition.

Day-old chicks need 35°C under the heat plate. Drop it by 3°C each week. A heat plate is safest. It acts like a broody hen and runs far cooler than a lamp. Cover the brooder floor in wood shavings. Start the chicks on
immediately at hatch; use a shallow lid or small tray for the first day rather than a deep feeder they can fall into.

Fully feathered chicks at five weeks can manage UK spring temperatures above 15°C in the day. Let them outside for a few hours on warm sunny mornings and bring them in at night. Keep them away from adult birds until week 10. Adult hens peck young pullets hard at introduction, and chicks vaccinated at hatch are not yet fully protected.
Phase 3: Grow-on (weeks 10 to point of lay)

From 8 weeks, switch to growers' pellets (around 16% protein, lower calcium than layer feed). Continue on growers until the first signs of approaching lay appear, red combs, widening pelvis, squatting behaviour. Then transition to layer pellets over one week. Introducing layer feed too early imposes excess calcium on kidneys that are not yet ready to process it; growers too late deprives the pullet of calcium for the first eggs.
Introducing pullets to the main flock

Wait until the pullets are close to the same size as the adult birds, usually around 16 to 18 weeks. Introduce at dusk by placing the pullets on the perch beside the existing flock while they are drowsy. Expect some pecking order disputes over the following two weeks; provide multiple feeding stations so pullets can eat without being driven off. Remove any bird that is being seriously victimised rather than waiting for the hierarchy to settle naturally.
